preamble: Affrication of a nasal consonant [n] (or palatal nasal [ɲ]) to voiced alveolar affricate [dz], typically in word-initial position conditioned by syllable structure. The articulatory mechanism involves the nasal closure acquiring an oral stop component whose release produces friction — a form of fortition known as denasalization-with-affrication. The most thoroughly documented case is in Southern Min (Hokkien), where Middle Chinese nasal initials regularly denasalized to voiced obstruents including [dz] in syllables with oral (non-nasal) codas.

examples.note: In Southern Min, Middle Chinese palatal nasal initials (*ɲ-, *nj-) denasalized to voiced affricates [dz] in syllables with oral codas. The word 日 (day) shows MC *ɲit → Hokkien dzit, and 二 (two, MC *njiH) → Hokkien dzi in conservative dialects. In many northern Hokkien varieties (urban Quanzhou, Amoy) this [dz] has further merged with /l/, but older speakers and Teochew preserve the affricate stage.

sources: Norman, J. (1988). Chinese. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics. Harcourt Brace.
